items.passage: Many migratory birds travel thousands of kilometers each year between their breeding and wintering grounds without losing their way. Scientists believe these birds are guided by several internal cues that keep them on course during long journeys. They appear to read the position of the sun and stars, while also sensing the planet's magnetic field through specialized cells. Younger birds often learn the safest routes by following experienced adults across unfamiliar terrain. Landmarks such as coastlines and mountain ranges further help them correct their direction along the way.

Carbon moves continuously between the atmosphere, oceans, soils, and living organisms in a process known as the carbon cycle. Green plants absorb carbon dioxide from the air and lock it into sugars during photosynthesis. When animals eat those leaves, the stored carbon passes along the food chain and returns to the sky through breathing. Decay and the burning of fossil fuels often release additional carbon that had been buried for millions of years. Oceans absorb much of this gas, although warmer water tends to hold less of it over time.

Selective attention allows people to focus on one important stream of information while ignoring competing sights and sounds around them. At a crowded party, for example, a listener can usually follow a single conversation despite the surrounding noise. This filtering happens because limited mental resources are assigned to the signals that seem to matter most at any moment. However, a sudden cue such as one's own name can break through the filter and capture notice instantly. Such findings suggest that ignored information is still processed quietly in the background.

items.subtopic: factory labor in the Industrial Revolution
items.passage: The Industrial Revolution transformed how ordinary people worked by moving production from small homes into large mechanized factories. Powered machines allowed a handful of operators to produce goods that once required many skilled artisans. As a result, workers were drawn from the countryside into rapidly growing industrial towns in search of wages. Although the pay was steady, long hours beside dangerous equipment became common, and entire families often labored together to survive. Over time, public concern about these harsh conditions encouraged the first laws regulating workplace safety.

Sedimentary rocks form gradually from layers of mud, sand, and the broken remains of older stone deposited by wind and water. Rivers usually carry tiny particles toward lakes and oceans, where they settle quietly onto the floor below. As fresh material piles up, the buried grains are pressed tightly together by the weight of the upper layers. Dissolved minerals then seep between the particles and slowly cement them into solid rock. Because these layers accumulate in order, geologists can often read them like pages recording the planet's history.

items.subtopic: the structure and tails of comets
items.passage: Comets are icy bodies that travel along stretched orbits carrying them from the cold outer solar system toward the Sun. Each comet has a small frozen core made of ice, dust, and trapped gases left over from the system's birth. As it approaches the Sun, the surface warms and often releases gas that forms a glowing cloud around the core. Pressure from sunlight and the solar wind then pushes this material outward, although the resulting tails are stretched into long, faint streams. After rounding the Sun, the comet retreats again and is gradually frozen until its next return.
